usage_notes: Often used to describe technology, trends, or cultural phenomena
Carries a sense of overwhelming prevalence
Can be neutral or slightly negative depending on context
examples: Smartphones have become ubiquitous in modern life.
The ubiquitous presence of surveillance cameras raises privacy concerns.

confusions.difference: 'Common' means frequently occurring; 'ubiquitous' means literally everywhere, inescapable.
'Pervasive' implies spreading through every part (often negative); 'ubiquitous' simply means everywhere.
